content.full_text:
City of National City
Office of the City Attorney
1243 National City Boulevard, National City, CA 92050-4397
George H.Elser, III—City Attorney                    (619) 338-4220


December 19, 1991


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California  95804

Re:  Request for Formal Written Advice or Alternatively for
     Informal Assistance

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

On behalf of National City Mayor George H. Waters, Councilman
Jessie E. Van Deventer and Councilwoman Rosalie G. Zarate, formal
written advice is hereby requested pursuant to Government Code
§83114(b), or alternatively, informal assistance is requested under
Government Code §83114.  I have been authorized to make this
request by action of the City Council.

By letter dated February 8, 1991, I requested advice on behalf of
Mayor George H. Waters, Councilman Michael R. Dalla, Councilman
Jessie E. Van Deventer, and Councilwoman Rosalie G. Zarate with
respect to an amendment (Amendment III) of the community's
redevelopment plan (the National City Downtown Redevelopment Plan).
These officials also serve as the chairman and members of the
community's redevelopment agency and housing commission, the
Community Development Commission of the City of National City ("the
CDC").

As set forth in my letter of February 8, the following were the
objectives of Amendment III:

     1.  Increasing the number of dollars of taxes which may
         be divided and allocated from $58 Million to $250
         Million;

     2.  Increasing the amount of bonded indebtedness which
         can be outstanding from $18.2 Million to $100
         Million;

DEC-19-'91 THU 17:44 ID:NATIONAL CITY                TEL.NO:FAX # 619 336-4376    #717 P02


                    December 19, 1991
                    Page 2


          3.    Extending the duration of the provisions, require-
                ments, and controls of the Redevelopment Plan from
                the year 2005 to 2015;

          4.    Pay debt service on existing bonds ($1.7 Million);

          5.    Make payments to other taxing agencies, namely, the
                County of San Diego, the National School District,
                and the Sweetwater Union High School District,
                probably through "pass-through" agreements (total
                approximate payments in excess of $8 Million);

          6.    20% set aside for low income housing;

          7.    Payment of overhead and administrative expenses;

          8.    Allocate the remaining funds one-half to neighbor-
                hood improvement, and one-half to commercial and
                industrial development;

          9.    Allowing payment by the CDC of part of the cost of
                acquiring land for and construction of a police
                facility.

     Due to the fact that there was a redevelopment plan amendment
involved, a major concern of ours in requesting advice was Section
18702.1(a)(3)(D) of the California Code of Regulations, which
defines "material financial effect" in the context of a redevelop-
ment plan amendment.

     In response to our request for advice, you furnished us with Advice
Letter A-91-098, dated April 15, 1991. The City and CDC proceeded
with Amendment III, enacting Ordinance No. 91-2013 on June 18,
1991, approving and adopting the amendment, and taking other
actions to accomplish the amendment. Based on your advice letter,
and prior to taking any of the actions related to Amendment III,
Mayor Waters, Councilman Van Deventer, and Councilman Zarette
announced their intention to abstain from participating in the
amendment. Since a quorum was then lacking, the City Council used
a random selection method to constitute a quorum, with Mayor Waters
being selected to participate.

     At present, the actions needed to accomplish the redevelopment plan
amendment have been accomplished. The City Council and CDC Board
are now about to consider actions to implement certain actions made
possible by Amendment III, such as the allocation of tax allocation
bonds, allocating funds to make neighborhood and industrial
improvements in the community, approval of tax increment "pass-

through" agreements with other taxing agencies, and the expenditure
of bonds in the low and moderate income housing fund to benefit
persons of low and moderate incomes. In discussing this matter
with you on the telephone, you have informed me that such "ministe-
rial" actions would not create a conflict of interest problem for
Mayor Waters, Councilman Van Daventsr, or Councilwoman Darce. I
have relayed this information to those officials, who have
requested that your advice be obtained in written form. According-
ly, we request that you furnish us your opinion on this matter in
the form of an advice letter, or if more appropriate, that you
treat this as a request for informal assistance and provide us with
a written opinion on that basis.

Very truly yours,

George H. Eiser, III
City Attorney

GHE/sbc
